data.title: SP500: The Trump Put Is Active, And Possibly The Fed Put
data.description:
The 20% selloff - no more?

The S&P500 (SP500) was supposed to crash on Monday - it was expected to be another black Monday. It was all about the punitive tariffs imposed on April 2nd. Then, on Friday China retaliated, which increased the risk of serious escalation. The risk was that the trade war escalation would lead to a global recession.

The S&P500 opened lower on Monday, at the bear market threshold, with the total drawdown below the 20% level. However, shortly after the market opened there was a rumor that Trump would delay the tariffs by 90 days - and the market spiked higher. However, the White House quickly denied the news as false news. In my opinion, that was the test on what would happen if Trump actually delays the tariffs.

My assessment was that the entire selloff was under control of a single man - the President, and he could reverse the selloff at any time. Further, the Monday low was likely the threshold that President Trump would tolerate, because once the bear market is confirmed, the selloff could intensify and cause a recession. .

On Tuesday, the S&P500 opened higher, but the rally faded due to further US-China trade war escalation. Yet, the Monday low point held, which supported my bottom thesis.

The bond market turmoil

Then on Tuesday night, the 10Y Treasury yield (US10Y) spiked over 4.5%. This was the key development.

On Monday, Japan requested an emergency meeting to make the deal - and this is very important. Specifically, Japan warned the US that it would not be able to continue to invest in US assets if the trade deficit with the US is eliminated.

Countries that have a trade deficit with the US use the US dollars earned from trade to invest in US assets, primarily the US Treasury Bonds (the US has a Capital Account surplus with these countries and the trade deficit matches the capital account surplus). Thus, the US trade partners with trade deficit essentially finance the US budget deficit.

Japan and China are the largest holders of US Treasury Bonds. Thus, Japan issued a warning that the reduction in trade deficit also means the reduction in capital account surplus - and that means the demand for US Treasury Bonds would sharply decrease, at the time when the US would need to issue a large amount to Treasuries to finance the deficit.

Thus, bond market investors got spooked - the US Treasury Bonds sold off, and the interest rate started to spike. A situation like this could lead to a serious financial crisis - with the capital outflow from the US, the crash in the US dollar (DXY), and collapse in the US economy as interest rates spike.

So, what happened next? Trump and his team realized that the situation is about to deteriorate and quickly acted - they delayed the reciprocal tariffs for 90 days, doing exactly what they tested on Monday.

As a result, the S&P500 spiked by 10% in a single day. This was the move I expected, and accordingly I issued a buy recommendation before the move happened.

The Trump Put

Thus, essentially, I have a weak hypothesis that Trump does not want to see the selloff exceed the 20% threshold. It is a weak hypothesis because Trump and his team indicated that they would tolerate a short-term selloff in stocks, and that could mean a selloff in excess of 20%.

However, Trump and his team cannot tolerate the turmoil in the bond market, as this could be a precursor to a major financial crisis. Thus, the Trump administration needs to deescalate the trade war, and there are early indications that there are deals in the making already. In fact, I expect an imminent deal announced with Japan - and this is likely to soothe the bond market.

Thus, as the bond market stabilizes, the stock market is likely to stabilize as well. In other words, the Trump Put is active. That means that any further financial instability is likely to lead to positive trade war news.

The Fed Put

The Fed was clear on many occasions that it will be patient before cutting interest rates - given the strength in the labor market, it can wait to see what the impact of tariffs on inflation will be. Thus, the market participants think that the Fed Put is not active, in other words the Fed would not cut interest rates if the stock market continues falling.

However, the Fed is also concerned about financial stability. I expected that if the Trump administration had failed to respond to the bond market turmoil, the Fed would have stepped in with the QE to buy Treasury Bonds. Given that the Trump administration reacted, the Fed did not have to. However, I suspect that the Fed is ready to step-in in case the bond market turmoil worsens, specifically, if the 10Y yields cross over 4.5%.

Implications

This is not the time to short the stock market, as President Trump can trigger a major rally with a single word, for example with the indication that he will engage in trade war negotiations with China.

This is a highly volatile market for now, and the best strategy is to buy the selloffs and possibly take profits with the spikes. Thus, I am playing the S&P500 (SPX) from the long side. This strategy will be appropriate until we get an early indication of an imminent recession - and at this point the hard data continues to be solid, especially for the labor market.

The risk is to the upside. I expect that the US-China trade war will deescalate, and the current 145% tariff on China will be significantly reduced, especially if the TikTok deal is made.

The current situation is still very fragile, and the selloff on Thursday, after the Wednesday's 10% rally, happened as the US Dollar sharply fell, especially against the Swiss Franc (FXF). The Swiss Franc is the ultimate safe haven currency, and inflows in Swiss Franck reveal the level of fear among international investors.

The Fed has a close eye on the US Dollar, and the Treasury Bonds, and the Trump team understands the danger of the situation. As long as the US labor market holds, that's positive for the S&P500 (SPY).

Here is the S&P500 5-day chart, showing the potential Monday bottom, at least for now. Obviously, the long position has to have a stop loss below Monday's low.

data.title: Dislocations Create Opportunities In High Yield
data.description:
By Bill Zox, CFA | John McClain, CFA | Jack Parker, CFA | S. Blake Haxton, JD, CFA | Dylan Herrmann, CFA

The high yield market has been increasingly dislocated in recent months, meaning some bonds were down much more while others held up much better than we believe was warranted. After the huge declines following the Trump administration’s April 2 tariff announcement, these dislocations are even more extreme. At the close on April 8, the ICE BofA US High Yield Index spread was 457 basis points, the yield-to-worst was 8.63%, and the dollar price was close to 92 cents.

Investors and allocators have to be "comfortable being uncomfortable." In periods of heightened volatility, the comfortable move is rarely the right one over time. We believe high yield is the safest risk asset class, and now is the time for allocators to add or make a plan to add high yield. Furthermore, haven assets like Treasuries and other sovereign bonds could potentially struggle in a pronounced stagflation environment in which deficit reduction targets are missed.

High Yield Weathers Well versus Equities

Some allocators are inclined to sell high yield when they fear recession. We encourage those allocators to focus first on their equity exposure. In significant stock market declines, high yield typically draws down less than stocks and recovers faster. After giving up all of their post-election gains in late March, stocks have been in freefall since the Trump administration’s unexpectedly extreme tariff announcement. While high yield bonds also have been under pressure, they have held up much better than stocks on a total return basis. From the peak of the Russell 2000 Index on November 25, 2024, through April 8, 2025, the High Yield Index is down a modest -2.0% while the Russell 2000 is down -27.9% on a total return basis. From the peak of the S&P 500 Index on February 19, 2025, high yield has declined only -3.3% versus an -18.9% drawdown for the S&P 500 (see Exhibits 1 and 2).

The resilience of high yield bonds relates to their seniority in the capital structure, low duration, high income, and contractual maturity date. At just over 3 years, high yield duration is low compared to stocks and core fixed income and also compared to its own historical range of 4 to 4.5 years. The contractual maturity date of a bond leads to a “pull to par,” which means that as long as default is avoided, the holder gets 100 cents on the dollar at maturity or, in some cases, more when the bond is retired prior to maturity. High yield bonds also generate meaningful current income that can be reinvested at higher yields when the market is weak.

As a result, high yield bonds are more tied to a company’s balance sheet. If the value of the business exceeds the face amount of the bonds, the business should be able to refinance the bonds so that the holder receives 100 cents on the dollar or more. Stocks more closely relate to the income and cash flow statements and the value that the market assigns to income and cash flows. This distinction was most apparent in the last decade for the S&P 500, which was actually 11 years (see Exhibit 3). From the beginning of 2000 to the end of 2010, the S&P 500 compounded at less than one-half of 1% per year, while the ICE BofA US High Yield Index compounded at over 7% per year. The S&P 500 did not catch up until the spring of 2021.

High Yield versus Leveraged Loans and Private Credit

As floating rate assets, leveraged loans and private credit have the wind at their back when the Federal Reserve is raising rates. When rate cuts are the focus, particularly due to a weakening economy, pressure will build on floating rate loans to below investment-grade companies. Lower rates mean lower yields on floating rate loans. In contrast, high yield bonds are fixed rate, and spread widening generally is cushioned by the decline in Treasury yields. From the February 19 peak in the S&P 500, the spread on the High Yield Index has widened by close to 200 basis points, yet the total return is down -3.3%, as of April 8, 2025. Some of that spread widening was cushioned by the 50-basis point decline in the 5-year Treasury yield since February 19.

Allocators Still Prefer CEOs and CFOs over Politicians

The demand for low duration credit of any kind, including high yield, has been strong since late 2023. This increased demand was met with muted net new supply as CEOs and CFOs focused more on preparing for potential recession and higher interest rates than aggressive capital allocation like debt-financed acquisitions, dividends, or stock buybacks. In effect, allocators want to bet on CEOs and CFOs rather than politicians. Inflows into high yield were high during 2024, even when spreads were narrow, because these allocations were strategic rather than tactical. We believe that in most cases these allocations were partial, with the plan to add when spreads widen or yields increase. So far, that is what we have seen in 2025 as inflows into high yield have continued at strong levels as spreads have widened.

Major Changes in Market Structure Create Opportunities

Systematic, momentum, and passive investors have been increasingly influential in the high yield market, particularly in recent weeks. In a down market, assume there are two reasonably comparable bonds, but one is down one point and the other is down five points. What explains the four-point price dislocation? The systematic player is looking for weak small-cap stocks of companies that also have high yield bonds. Upon any fundamental hiccup, the systematic investor typically would short the stock and bonds, putting the company into a negative feedback loop. The stock is sold because the bonds are weak, and the bonds are sold because the stock is weak. Meanwhile, the passive player is a seller of both bonds equally to fund redemptions and replicate the benchmark. In this hypothetical example, the momentum player would short the bond that has sold off more because it is going down. Lastly, the active manager sells the bond that is down one point to buy the bond that is down five points. In recent weeks, however, the market has been dominated by the systematic, momentum, and passive players while the active managers have been less willing to step up.

Risk Mitigation and Opportunity Identification

We are more cognizant of our exposure to the high yield bonds of public small-cap companies that have experienced fundamental hiccups. In our view, the prevalence of systematic, momentum, and passive investors in the high yield and equity markets in recent weeks has created dislocations and a negative feedback loop, particularly for companies with weak small-cap stocks that also have high yield bonds. In a situation like the current environment, we focus on risk mitigation and opportunity identification.

We have always preferred the transparency, price discovery, access to capital, and discipline of the public stock market. What we have found is that when a stock is down 25% to 60% in a quarter, the CEO’s family and friends, the Board, employees, customers, and suppliers all know it. Often, the CEO’s highest priority is to restore the confidence of the bond market to break the negative feedback loop of declining stock and bond prices. We believe this dynamic creates opportunities for active high yield investors. In contrast, we believe that CEOs of private companies are more likely to keep their foot on the accelerator because their stock valuation is not widely known. This thesis, which generally has served us well over time, will be tested in the coming quarters, but we are confident that it is justified.

In the middle of 2022, the High Yield Index bottomed at about a 600 basis-points spread and 9% yield. We believe that was a market that was priced for a very high probability of recession. We started to see capital from various sources, including some private equity capital, come into the high yield market. The High Yield Index has compounded at 8.2% since then. In late October of 2023, the High Yield Index was at about a 450 basis-points spread and 9.5% yield, and the market took off from there. The High Yield Index has compounded at 10.5% since then. We believe we are approaching a similar point today. Further, we continue to see opportunities, and we believe there are plenty of safe 7% bonds, lots of strong 8% to 9% bonds, and some really interesting 10% to 12% bonds, which could have a materially higher internal rate of return. With the slowdown in earnings power, we find these yields compelling in an asset class that is tied to the balance sheet more than the income or cash flow statement like equities.

Nobody knows where current events will settle, but the financial markets, elected officials, the courts, public opinion, and plenty of other forces will come into play. This environment feels to us like one that leads to attractive returns for the long-term investor.

Index Definitions

The ICE BofA US High Yield Index tracks the performance of USD-denominated below investment grade corporate debt publicly issued in the major domestic markets.

The Russell 2000® Index, which is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index, measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes approximately 2,000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market capitalization and current index membership.

The S&P 500 Index is a broad measure of U.S. domestic large cap stocks. The 500 stocks in this capitalization-weighted index are chosen based on industry representation, liquidity, and stability.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SoFi Technologies: A Compelling Accumulation Opportunity
data.description:
I wrote on SoFi (NASDAQ:SOFI) recently stating why the investment case was so complicated and made the case that it was prudent to take some profits. Since that time, the S&P is down about 15% while SoFi is down over 35%. Admittedly, it was lucky timing to write a Sell rated article prior to significant policy related volatility, but SoFi was clearly expensive. Much of the bear case that I discussed remains: a declining rate environment, slower lending growth, high default rates, and a cannibalization of its competitive advantage. Yet, the valuation has come back to an attractive level and its now much more sensible to expect positive investment returns in both the short and long-term.

Buy the Dip

The recent selloff offers a very compelling opportunity to begin or add to an existing SoFi position. The company trades at roughly 40x forward profit before tax while expecting 22% forward sales growth and a continued inflection in profitability. Business metrics remain exceptional, with 26% YoY net revenue growth, 34% YoY user base growth, and 32% YoY growth in total products underscored by $23.2b in loan originations. Deposit funding was solid with $25b in deposits, while continued traction in both technology platforms and capital market deals provided a very stable base upon which SoFi can grow.

SoFi is putting on a masterclass on how to win and grow in the digital banking era. The company has no brick & mortar footprint, rather uses a mobile native banking app as its top-of-funnel customer acquisition driver. Users can expect a similar suite of banking products that a traditional bank would offer with the sleek interface and ease of use of a modern Fintech app. Over time, new products and services will be used to create deeper customer relationships and much stickier deposits. Acquiring customers with a low-value, zero cost product and then cross-selling them into higher margin products is not a new concept. In fact, all of SoFi's Fintech peers have taken the same strategy. It works though, and SoFi is executing on the strategy well. The company's Financial Services segment revenue has grown from $167m in 2022 to $821m in 2024. Contribution profit for the segment improved by $500m over that time to reach $300m.

Meanwhile, the company is building very durable revenue streams with its Technology segment, which offers modern backend infrastructure to legacy banks. The product-market fit for Technisys and Galileo is very real. The demand profile for these infrastructure solutions has echoes of the oft quoted and unfortunately true statement that "most of American finance runs on Excel 2003". It may be a tongue-in-cheek comment, but it's woefully true. It's prohibitively difficult to update back-end banking infrastructure in a timely, safe, and cost-effective way. SoFi simplifies this process for enterprise customers (competing banks or Fintechs), which is another key point I brought up in my bearish article earlier this year.

SoFi is effectively modernizing its banking segment competitors with the offerings in its technology segment. The risk is clear: legacy banks have better scale, better brand name recognition, and more capital than SoFi. The entire bull case of Fintech is that modern competitors have an edge in efficiency, driving down cost to serve, which should lead to better unit economics than traditional banks over time. By offering modernized back-end infrastructure solutions to these very banks, SoFi is offering up this competitive advantage in exchange for a durable revenue stream. Product parity with legacy incumbents has a material impact on SoFi's competitive advantage, but the discussion really ought to be focused on whether the revenue stream is worth the loss of that edge. I see it as a risk worth noting but don't believe it will materially harm SoFi. Traditional banks will work to modernize their infrastructure one way or the other, and this business segment at least allows SoFi to make money on this.

Meanwhile, relative to its Fintech peers, SoFi has a much smaller user base. The company has roughly 10 million 'members', compared to PayPal (PYPL) with 434 million users, Venmo with 60 million users, and Block's (XYZ) Cash App with 57 million users. Robinhood (HOOD) has about 20 million users and 25 million funded accounts. Granted, the value of the average SoFi user is likely higher than those competitors, since those apps use a free service as their top of funnel acquisition driver.

The market is clearly undecided. SoFi has a very solid margin profile now that it's a profitable company but also carries a premium multiple relative to the incumbent banks that it's modernizing. It has a very elevated short interest, over 10%, illustrating investor uncertainty. Meanwhile, the company regularly posts double beats and raises in earnings prints, but also regularly trades down post-earnings on sandbagged guidance. The stock has been terribly frustrating to invest despite very solid underlying business performance, but that shouldn't shake out long-term investors. Underlying business growth will result in positive stock returns over time.

My opinion is this: SoFi is a clear Fintech leader with a very differentiated product stack. The user base is sticky, growing, and young. SoFi benefits from its user base becoming richer over time, which begins a flywheel with its increasing suite of banking products. This is a winning equation. Meanwhile, SoFi is becoming a powerhouse in consumer lending and continues happily selling loan books to larger banks to shore up capital. A higher for longer rate environment benefits lenders, but a recession would decrease lending demand. The investment case in SoFi remains very complicated, but I see a positive risk/reward at current levels as the premium multiple is more than justified by premium growth relative to incumbent banking peers. The recent poor price performance means little to the company's long-term story.

The biggest risks remain twofold: 1) SoFi's technology segment allows larger, scaled competitors to reach product parity, and 2) SoFi has a very high default rate relative to legacy banks. That said, I feel management manages default risk appropriately and is not lending irresponsibly. Therefore I do not see default rates evolving into a serious issue for the company. As the user base grows older and becomes higher earning on average, this issue should naturally sort itself out.

Investor Takeaway

The future of banking is digital, and SoFi is a clear leader in digital banking services. That structural tailwind will remain through macro headwinds or recessionary conditions, so there's plenty of reason to believe that SoFi can continue reliably compounding its user base. The financial benefits will follow.

I'm upgrading SoFi back to a Buy as the recent dip presents an opportune time to accumulate this stock.

data.title: SCHX: Stock Market Crash Offers A Great Buying Opportunity For Hefty Gains
data.description:
The stock market crash has created attractive buying opportunities for investors who can tolerate short term headwinds to earn lofty returns over the long term. The selloff has cooled valuations from the recent peak while the potential rate cuts are likely to fuel the rebound in the coming months. Schwab U.S. Large-Cap ETF™ (NYSEARCA:SCHX) appears to be a solid investment vehicles to take advantage of the selloff. This is because the ETF offers access to a portfolio of more than 700 stocks through a single investment. Its low expense ratio, solid liquidity, decent yield and a low risk factor makes it a perfect ETF for buy and hold strategy. Therefore, I initiate coverage of SCHX with a buy rating.

Stock Market Crash Never Lasts for Longer

The US stock market plummeted sharply amid a trade war, sending NASDAQ into a bear market and erasing 17% of the S&P 500 value. The downtrend is expected to last for the next few months because the US market is likely to experience a recession in 2025.

Economists are forecasting a higher probability of a recession. For example, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley declined their 2025 GDP growth outlook to 1.5% and 1.3%, respectively. Both have also increased odds of a recession to the highest levels in the past two years. Morningstar estimate a 40 to 50% chance of a recession. Moreover, Wall Street continues to slash earnings growth expectations for the first quarter and full year. According to the FactSet data, the S&P 500 earnings are likely to grow around 7% in the first quarter, compared to initial expectations for a double-digit increase.

The situation in the capital and credit market is also challenging. Reports suggest uncertainty has significantly declined activity in the IPO and M&A markets. Furthermore, the banks are expected to report lower earnings for the first quarter of 2025 with expectations that slower economic growth could increase credit losses, decline lending activity and reduce business confidence.

Apparently, the market trends appear scary. However, I believe investors with a high risk tolerance should consider buying beaten down stocks and ETFs during the current bear market. Historical data suggest that bear market lasts on average around 9.9 months, while bull trends can continue for years. The average decline in the past bear markets was 35%. In fact, the stock market bounced back and soared to new highs even after the most painful selloff of the Great Depression and Financial Crisis.

I believe the cheap valuations and rate cuts are likely to fuel a recovery in the coming months and quarters. In the scenario of a slower economic growth, the Fed is expected to slash rates at a faster pace than initial expectations. Goldman Sachs expects a 130 basis points rate cut if the economy enters a recession. In 2020, the Fed adopted a zero interest rate policy to counter recession, which helped in increasing investor confidence and quickly recovering the losses.

Furthermore, the US stock market valuations now appear attractive around 18x forward earnings, compared to the recent peak of 22.5x. The tech sector and magnificent 7 are also trading slightly over 21x earnings, compared to their recent levels of 30x forward earnings. Lower valuations are also likely to improve investor sentiment in risky stocks.

SCHX is a Solid Investment Option

There are various ways to capitalize on the selloff while lowering the risk factors. The strategy of creating a portfolio of few beaten down stocks could be one of the common ways of capitalizing on the potential uptrend while lowering the downside risk. Moreover, considering growth or sector-focused ETFs may also enable investors to earn healthy returns in the recovery. Nevertheless, considering investment options, such as Schwab U.S. Large-Cap ETF, could be a great strategy. This is because SCHX offers access to a large number of stocks through a single investment.

Unlike growth-focused ETFs with a higher exposure to tech stocks, SCHX offers a better portfolio structure, with technology representing 30% of the overall portfolio. The communications and consumer cyclical collectively make up 20% of its portfolio. Whereas, the rest of its 50% of portfolio is spread across the remaining eight sectors. A diversified portfolio always helps in lowering the volatility and other risk factors.

The fund's 31% of portfolio concentration in the top 10 holdings limits its exposure to a single or a group of few stocks. SCHX offers exposure to popular names like Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Tesla (TSLA), Broadcom (AVGO and nearly 740 other stocks. Furthermore, its portfolio offers a solid combination of growth, value and dividend stocks. According to the SCHX data, if one had invested $10,000 in SCHX in 2015, his investment would have grown to $31,500. This represents a massive 215% return on investment.

The recent share price selloff has made its valuations attractive, trading around 18x forward earnings. SCHX share price is currently trading around the 52 weeks low of $19 per share. In addition, after the 3 for 1 stock split in late 2024, the fund’s lower per-share price can enable investors buy shares in a large quantity. Besides the attractiveness of the share price, SCHX offers low expense ratio, healthy liquidity and a decent yield.

SCHX also earned the strong grades on expenses, dividends and liquidity, according to the Seeking Alpha quant grading system. Its A grade on the risk factor also vindicates my opinion about the ETF’s potential to generate better risk-adjusted returns. SCHX received a hold rating based on the quant score of 2.83. The hold rating is mainly blamed on its low momentum score due to the recent share price selloff.

Other investment options with diversified portfolios are iShares Dow Jones U.S. ETF (IYY), iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB) and Vanguard Russell 1000 Index Fund ETF Shares (VONE). iShares Dow Jones U.S. ETF tracks the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Index. Its portfolio is composed of 1000 stocks, while iShares Russell 1000 ETF and Vanguard Russell 1000 Index Fund ETF Shares track the performance of Russell 1000 index. I prefer SCHX over peers due to its low expense ratio, high liquidity and low per-share price.

In Conclusion

Legendary investors like Warren Buffett have made big profits by investing in a crisis situation and holding those investments for a long time. I believe the current selloff offers one of the greatest buying opportunities in the last two years. However, choosing the right investment option is critical in lowering the risk factor while capitalizing on the potential recovery. SCHX appears to be a solid investment option because of its diversified portfolio, low expense ratio and healthy liquidity.

data.title: Equity Outlook: Finding Silver Linings In Very Cloudy Markets
data.description:
By Nelson Yu

As volatility rises, staying invested is a strategic priority for capturing long-term return potential in a broadening market.

Global equities faced fresh challenges in the first quarter of 2025 amid growing trade-war concerns and developments in artificial intelligence. Bouts of volatility and an increasingly cloudy outlook underscored the importance of focusing on diversification, valuations and company fundamentals.

After a promising start to the year, global equities lost momentum in February and March (Display). The MSCI ACWI Index of global developed- and emerging-market stocks fell by 1.3% in US-dollar terms during the first quarter, as regional returns diverged. Market returns broadened, as evidenced by the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index’s outperformance of the cap-weighted S&P 500.

Regional Returns Diverged in Volatile First Quarter

Since the end of the quarter, headlines have been dominated by the unprecedented tariff policies. Even before the sweeping announcements on April 2 and the recent pause, US policy uncertainty rattled markets as President Donald Trump zigzagged but ultimately imposed levies on all imports from China and on select goods from Mexico, Canada and the European Union. During the first quarter, market volatility was also fueled by the Chinese company DeepSeek’s unveiling of a new AI model in January that undermined confidence in the earnings prospects of the Magnificent Seven US mega-caps.

Regional, Sector and Style Trends Shifted

The lack of clarity around trade policy prompted a divergence in regional returns. In a sharp reversal from long-standing trends, European and emerging-market stocks outpaced US equities during the quarter by a wide margin (Display, above). The MSCI Europe ex UK Index gained 10.7% in US-dollar terms. By contrast, US large-caps—the clear market victors in recent years—gave ground, with the S&P 500 down 4.3%.

Continuing a trend that began late last year, the dominance of the US mega-caps waned. DeepSeek’s cost-efficient model raised questions about future AI spending, triggering a sell-off of the Magnificent Seven that dragged technology stocks down 11.6% (Display). Energy and utilities led sector gains in the quarter, while healthcare stocks rebounded from a weak 2024. Lower-volatility stocks did well as investors sought safe havens. Value stocks, which have underperformed growth stocks in recent years, also benefited in the broadening market.

Sector and Style Trends Shifted as Technology and Growth Underperformed

The Complex Effects of a Trade War

The policy conundrums that have dominated headlines aren’t going away soon. In particular, the Trump administration’s on-again, off-again approach to tariffs has left import-dependent US firms susceptible to volatile price swings, while exporters around the world also feel the effects. Even after Trump announced a 90-day pause for tariffs on most countries (excluding China) on April 9, many companies will struggle to advance investment plans while the trade regime remains fluid. Uncertainty might ease if a more systematic approach to tariffs takes root, which could quell investor anxiety, despite concern about the economic fallout of a trade war.

Trade strains and geopolitical risk, from hostilities in Ukraine and the Middle East to China-Taiwan tensions, have threatened to rekindle inflation and muddied the interest-rate outlook. During the first quarter, the US Federal Reserve and Bank of Japan held off on monetary easing, while the European Central Bank cut its benchmark rate 50 basis points to 2.5%—near its long-term target.

For economies and companies, the outcomes of a prolonged trade war will be complex. For example, even Trump has acknowledged that the US could face a recession. That said, the US economy isn’t particularly trade-sensitive, and some companies are more tariff-resistant than others, depending on their industry and operational footprint. Some global companies with US operations might even benefit from tariffs. Examples include European and Japanese electronics manufacturers and consumer goods companies with a large US manufacturing presence.

Expanding Investment Horizons

Investors, too, face complicated challenges with policy uncertainty at a near record high while equity markets broaden. We think the time is right to reevaluate style and regional exposures, particularly as many investors became overweight US growth stocks in recent years because of the Magnificent Seven’s prior dominance.

Defensive equities could add breadth and fortitude. By investing in high-quality stocks with stable trading patterns, a defensive strategy can help reduce volatility created by political, technology and macroeconomic risks. Value stocks offer diversification and still trade at deep discounts to growth stocks. While value stocks are perceived as vulnerable to economic cycles, we believe investors can find undervalued stocks with attractive free cash flows and business attributes that can drive earnings, even in a more sluggish economic environment.

Regional diversification deserves attention. European equities may finally be finding their footing, even as the Russia-Ukraine war remains an ongoing risk. Equity valuations in Europe are still attractive versus US peers, and the average European company beat consensus expectations by 3% during fourth-quarter earnings season. There are even signs of a reversal in outflows from European stock funds over the last three years. Selective investors can find European companies with consistent earnings growth and quality businesses that are more resilient to regional macro and geopolitical risks.

Emerging markets are also showing signs of life and offer hidden opportunities. Some of the biggest players in the global artificial intelligence supply chain are based in emerging markets. Earnings estimates are trending upward across the developing world, which accounts for 90% of the world’s population and roughly half of global GDP. And our research shows that missing out on an EM recovery could be costly for investors.

Is a Realignment of Earnings Underway?

Recent shifts in equity return patterns call for a closer look at longer-term earnings trends. Over the last 15 years, US corporate earnings growth has outpaced that of non-US companies, represented by the MSCI EAFE Index. However, before 2010, that wasn’t always the case. In fact, our research shows that in three of the four decades since 1970, non-US earnings exceeded US earnings (Display).

Earnings in Historical Perspective: US Companies Haven’t Always Led

This year, US corporate earnings growth is expected to come closer in line with earnings growth of the rest of the world. Meanwhile, equity valuations outside the US are still at a meaningful discount, based on 2025 forecast earnings (Display).

Earnings Growth Is Expected to Converge, Yet Valuation Gaps Remain Wide

To be sure, even after a tough quarter, we think US stocks remain integral to any diversified allocation. For the last 60 years, US earnings growth has consistently risen, overcoming major economic and geopolitical shocks. Long-standing US advantages—from innovation to education to corporate culture—remain a potent force for equity returns. But how you access those returns matters. As we see it, a US allocation with disciplined active portfolios across the style spectrum is the right way to tap into a changing market.

Sharpening Focus on the “Magnificent Others”

Steep first-quarter declines of the Magnificent Seven have reminded us of the risks of concentrated positions. While the mega-caps include world-class companies at the forefront of the AI revolution, even great companies can pose investing risk. We believe each name should be held in accordance with a portfolio’s philosophy and at appropriate weights, as disruptors themselves can be disrupted.

The unprecedented events around trade policy also remind us that in-depth company research is critical to uncovering opportunities to generate long-term wealth. In our view, investors today must strive to understand the resilience of corporate margins in the face of supply and growth shocks, as well as the impact of other disruptive forces like DeepSeek on the AI megacaps in the US. We continue to see attractive opportunities among what we call the Magnificent Others—high-quality businesses scattered across sectors and styles that have sound balance sheets and consistent earnings streams.

Zooming Out on Volatility: Perspective Matters

Of course, diversified portfolios aren’t immune to volatility. Yet, we think some perspective can help investors cope with turbulent markets, after the S&P 500’s peak-to-trough decline of 10.1% in the first quarter of 2025.

In any given calendar year, US stock investors can expect to see sizable negative moves. In fact, since 1980 the S&P has experienced an intra-year peak-to-trough drawdown of 14%, on average. Despite market disruptions—from economic crises to a once-in-a-generation pandemic—the S&P 500 finished higher in 36 of the past 44 years (Display).

US Stocks Have Risen in Most Years Despite Intra-Year Corrections

The past quarter has been instructive on many fronts. We've seen that valuations do matter, as shares of the expensive mega-caps pulled back. Another lesson: consensus views can be dramatically wrong, such as the conventional wisdom at the beginning of the year that Trump’s policies would deliver an unequivocal win for US businesses and stocks.

In this changing environment, maintaining a diverse geographic portfolio can help cast a wider net for earnings growth — which can also help investors cope with inflation. Sticky inflation means our assets must work harder to generate returns, and inflation typically leads to higher nominal earnings, which drive stock market returns. That’s why equities have a long history of delivering positive real returns, above the inflation rate.

We’ve also been reminded that volatility is part and parcel of long-term equity investing—and diversification is one of the best antidotes. Investors should gird for more market turbulence and policy uncertainty, particularly around the pace, level and industries affected by tariffs. But there are silver linings in this year’s cloudy market: a less-concentrated equity backdrop that could reward patient equity investors who remain focused on finding companies with strong fundamentals, and more attractive valuations for those who stay invested to achieve their long-term goals.

data.title: Oil States International Is Sufficiently Strong Despite The Recent Fall
data.description:
OIS Keeps It Moving

I discussed Oil States International (NYSE:OIS) in the past, and you can read the latest article here, published on January 3. At the start of 2025, OIS’s strategic priorities revolve around rapid geographic diversification and focusing on growth areas in offshore and onshore. The flow of offshore projects in the Eastern Hemisphere and Latin America will keep the ball rolling. Recently, it has had success in introducing perforating systems and digital technologies. I think the company’s operating margin will take a higher trajectory by the end of the year.

However, the recent tariff hikes and global crude oil demand concerns can suppress its performance in the near term. The cash flow deterioration is worrying, but a robust balance sheet should protect it from any sudden shock. After a 25% fall in the past month, the stock appears undervalued compared to its peers; so, I maintain my “Buy” call with an expectation of a higher return in the medium term.

Why Do I Keep My Call Unchanged?

In my previous iteration, published on January 3, I discussed how OIS was streamlining operations, exiting underperforming locations, and introducing new products. Despite global crude oil demand concerns and cash flow deterioration, the company’s strategic initiatives and higher backlog for subsea production systems support a "Buy" rating. I wrote:

OIS is streamlining its US operations by exiting underperforming locations and business lines, including drilling, flowback, and well-testing operations. With a bullish long-term outlook, it expects a higher backlog for subsea production systems. It has secured long-term contracts with its international customers for perforating products. It also sees traction in awards in Latin America and the Eastern Hemisphere in 2025. Its recent collaboration with Seadrill Limited in offshore MPD operations can generate additional associated revenues in 2025.

With significant international exposure and large awards coming from Latin America and the Eastern Hemisphere, I think OIS is better poised to benefit in 2025. On the other hand, the global economy is facing hurdles in demand and supply. Crude oil prices have gone south. Cash flows have deteriorated. The stock’s relatively cheap valuation warrants that I continue to assign my “Buy” rating.

Current Growth Drivers

OIS’s management sees an encouraging energy market where robust demand will drive the capital investments. I see various projects in development in Latin America, the United States, Asia, and Africa. I see various FIDs with production orders with major subsea OEMs (other equipment manufacturers). In this environment, the company’s sales of managed pressure drilling systems ticked up in South America in Q4. Also, its conductor connector products and the FlexJoint deepwater riser connector products will see a higher demand.

In the shallow water, opportunities have emerged in subsea pipeline and associated repair systems. Here, OIS has an advantage because some of its global manufacturing operations have low capital intensity, providing it with low operating costs. However, the market conditions have slightly weakened following the US tariff war and the global uncertainty in the industry. In the OFS market, though, I do not think it will be significantly impacted. So, the company’s Completion and Production Services and Downhole Technology segment can see activity levels remaining relatively flat.

As I discussed in my previous articles, the company has introduced new technologies like open architecture perforating systems and digital technologies for wireline service. In the international market, it secured contracts for perforating products, particularly in Latin America and the Eastern Hemisphere, which are expected to roll in 2025.

C&P Business And Offshore Prospects

The Downhill Technologies business was weak through November and December due to the holiday and typical seasonality. Despite that, OIS managed to improve profitability in this business as the international expansion efforts started paying off and new international contract opportunities arrived. At the same time, it will start rolling out its products in the US market, which can have a positive effect.

Another important growth driver for OIS is the offshore production facilities, specifically FPSOs. Such facilities demonstrated strong growth in Brazil, Guyana, and more recently, Suriname. Investors may note Petrobras is one of the largest deepwater drillers and producers. However, OIS’s market share appears to be shrinking in Southeast Asia because of shipping products from a higher manufacturing cost base. To reduce operating costs, it is building a manufacturing facility in that region.

Company Outlook

OIS’s management is cautiously optimistic about OIS’s outlook. At the company’s guidance mid-point, revenues remain unchanged while adjusted EBITDA can decline in Q1’25 compared to Q4’24. It expects to generate cash flows from operations in the range of $65 million to $75 million.

Given the outlook and the potential for incremental margin improvement in the US. In FY2025, OIS’s revenues can increase by 4% while its adjusted EBITDA can increase by 17.6% compared to FY2024.

My Estimates

Over the past 14 quarters, OIS's adjusted EBITDA increased by 10%, on average. Given the current growth drivers, I expect the EBITDA to secure a slightly higher growth trajectory as its topline growth also accelerates. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 13%-15%.

Analyzing Q4 Performance

As announced in the Q4 earnings released on February 21, OIS’s Completion and Production Services segment revenues decreased by 25% quarter-over-quarter in Q4 2024. The segment adjusted EBITDA margin contracted by 170 basis points in Q4. I discussed the company’s restructuring initiatives, including the exit of two underperforming service offerings and the closure of several facilities in the US, in my previous article. It resulted in revenue loss, asset impairment charges, and other costs associated with the exit, reducing its operating profit margin in Q4.

The Offshore Manufactured Products segment, on the other hand, witnessed 5% higher revenue in Q4, while its adjusted EBITDA margin remained steady. The Downhole Technologies segment revenue decreased (by 15%) in Q4, while its adjusted EBITDA margin contracted by 290 basis points.

Cash Flows And Leverage

In FY2024, OIS's cash flow from operations declined by 19% compared to a year ago due primarily to an increase in inventories and a decrease in accounts payable and accrued cash incentive compensation. As a result, its free cash flow (or FCF) dried up by 68% in FY2024. OIS's debt-to-equity ratio was 0.18x as of December 31, 2024. It had $123 million in liquidity as of that date. I expect cash flows to decline in Q1 because the timing of funding short and long-term incentive payments typically affects cash flows adversely in Q1.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

OIS's forward EV/EBITDA multiple contraction versus the current EV/EBITDA is significantly steeper than its peers, implying a much higher EBITDA growth. This typically reflects in a higher EV/EBITDA multiple than its peers. Its EV/EBITDA multiple (5.7x) is lower than its peers' (OII, RNGR, and FTI) average. So, the stock is undervalued compared to its peers. It is also trading at a steep discount to its five-year average.

OIS's five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple is 10.2x. If the stock trades at this multiple, its stock price can increase by 1.6x from the current level. The calculation is based on its current (or TTM) EBITDA of $62 million and multiplying the EBITDA by the five-year multiple. Then, we deduct net debt from it to arrive at market capitalization and divide it by the weighted average share count to get the target price. The average EV/EBITDA multiple for OIS's peers (OII, RNGR, FTI) is 6.7x. If the stock trades at this average, the stock rises by 59%. Since my last publication in August, where I suggested a "Buy," the stock has decreased by approximately 31%.

I expect the offshore projects and OIS’s product introductions to start benefiting in the medium term. So, I expect 13%- 15% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next 12 months (as I already discussed). Feeding these values in the EV calculation and assuming the sell-side EV/EBITDA multiple of 5.7x, I think the stock should trade between $6.6 and $6.7, implying a ~82% upside.

Analyst Rating

According to Seeking Alpha, three sell-side analysts rated OIS a "Buy" (includes "Strong buy"). One analyst rated it a “Hold.” None ranked it as a "sell." The consensus target price is $6.8, which yields 85% returns at the current price. Based on my estimates, the Wall Street analysts have fairly reasonable expectations from the stock.

What’s The Call On OIS?

As OIS streamlines its US operations and focuses more on international operations, the share of international revenues grew to 72%, while US onshore revenues were restricted to 28% of its total sales portfolio. With various subsea projects rolling in Latin America, Asia, and Africa, and major investments being tied in, I think OIS will gather steam going forward. Particularly, demand for its system conductor connector and deepwater riser connector can rise.

International expansion and offshore production facilities helped expand OIS’s operating margin in Q4. However, the outlook for Completion and Production (including downhole technologies) appears to have been diminished following recent events. Its cash flows deteriorated in FY 2024. So, the stock underperformed VanEck Vectors Oil Services ETF (OIH) in the past year. I expect the company’s cash flow to fall further in the next couple of quarters, but it should bounce back by the end of FY2025 as the company cashes in on the opportunities. I suggest investors continue to “Buy” it.

data.title: Rates Spark: U.S. Yields Not For Turning, So Spreads Widen
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Benjamin Schroeder, Michiel Tukker

US Treasury yields in a mood to re-test higher in the period ahead

The US 30yr auction was strong. It came some 2.5bp through (effectively rich to secondary). The indirect bid was strong (mostly foreign). The direct bid (more domestic) too. Dealer need to support the issue in consequence was low, so all good on the auction metrics, and this is a vote of positivity on Treasuries. It's likely that a perception of value helped, as the 30yr yield rose in the hours into the auction. Still, a strong 30yr auction following a strong 10yr auction is quite a good look, especially in the wake of a really poor 3yr auction where central banks were expected to show up and lap up paper.

Despite this, we're inclined to view higher yields as an ongoing risk ahead. We've had too much risk-off in recent days that has not correlated with lower yields. So, there is still an implicit negative tint attached to Treasuries. That's not shaken yet, and the 10yr swap spread is re-widening too. It had hit 60bp, before collapsing back down towards 50bp yesterday on the "pause" news. But it has since edged back up towards 55bp.

Directionally, there seems to be no mood to materially test lower in yield right now, despite the open door being presented by the risk-off backdrop. Our impression from here is the 10yr Treasury yield will have a tendency to head for the 4.5% area, potentially with a view to thinking about 4.75%.

The March US budget numbers are not awful, but still not great. The running deficit has widened to US$1.3tn. That compares with US$1.1tn on an adjusted basis for the same period of fiscal year 2024 (October to March). The detail showed a moderate rise in tariff revenue (only small though). Cumulative revenues are running at 3.3% above 2024, while spending is running at 9.7% above. The good news is the monthly deficit at US$160bn is lower than the US$237bn seen for the same month last year. Spending was lower by 7.1% compared with March last year. That helped to narrow the running deficit to 15% above 2024. Overall, some improvement in March, but the cumulative numbers remain troubling.

EUR markets remain cautious after the US U-turn

It is sinking in that the US will still impose stiff tariffs despite Trump’s announcement, and while the 90-day delay gives room for negotiations and deals, it will remain a greater time of uncertainty, which by itself can have a chilling effect on economic activity. So, it was no surprise that the knee-jerk increase in EUR rates as a reaction to Trump’s U-turn on tariffs faded relatively quickly.

The European Central Bank pricing is now somewhat less extreme - in that there is no longer a probability discounted in forwards of a 50bp April cut - but overall, the market is still firmly looking for three cuts from the ECB this year. 2y swap rates ended only some 2bp higher. There was, however, a larger move higher in front-end Schatz yields which is a reversal of the prior safe haven demand as the most severe market turmoil in risk assets now has an end. The Schatz yield is still trading some 19bp below swaps compared to around 15bp just before "Liberation Day", and thus, still reflects remaining market caution.

Longer rates dropped, mirroring to some degree the much larger bull-flattening moves seen in US SOFR and Sterling SONIA swap curves - 30y SONIA OIS rates, for instance, dropped by 16bp, mostly reversing the prior day’s 20bp surge. But staying in EUR space, Bund yields ended the day little changed versus Wednesday, i.e., before Trump's announcement, though this implies Bund yields trading back above swaps. At the same time, we are not back to the 5bp to 10bp above swaps area yet, also here reflecting caution. This is also confirmed by EGB spreads over Bunds still noticeably above pre-Liberation Day levels.

Dutch pension funds no longer needing vote in adjusted legal proposal

Whilst Dutch pension funds feel the pain from lower equity prices, the coalition party NSC is still trying to change the rules of the current reforms. In the latest proposal, each pension fund would no longer be required to obtain a vote of approval before transitioning from a defined benefits system to a defined contributions system. Instead, pension funds would have to offer the participants the possibility to opt out from the transition of his or her assets to the new system.

This change to the amendment addresses some of the issues identified by the stakeholders in earlier consultation phases, but will likely still face strong opposition. The risk-sharing characteristic of the Dutch pension fund system cannot be guaranteed if each participant gets a choice about which model to follow. Having individuals choose whether to transition their assets could lead to a self-selection bias, which would have negative outcomes to certain groups (e.g., younger participants).

Having said that, the tail risk of significant amounts of assets remaining under the old system seems to be mitigated, and thus, the potential impact on swap markets should be limited. Even if the amendment makes it through Parliament and the Senate, we think only a small number of participants will likely actively opt out from the transition. A vote for approval would have been much more risky, as this could have implied an entire fund, e.g. ABP with €500bn+ AUM, would be prevented from transitioning. As such, we think funds will continue to prepare for the upcoming reforms by adjusting their interest rate hedges.

Thursday's events and market views

From the UK we first have the flash GDP estimate for February, which is expected to show a 0.1% month-on-month growth. The US will publish PPI numbers, but given the limited reaction to the undershooting of CPI data, we don't think markets will pay much attention. The Michigan indices may actually get more attention, as these have a stronger forward-looking component. We also have the Fed's William speak on the monetary policy outlook.

In terms of supply, we have Italy with a 3y, 6y, 7y and 13y BTP auction for a total of €9bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Intuitive Surgical: Premium Valuation Meets Mounting Headwinds (Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Intuitive Surgical (NASDAQ:ISRG) stock is off 2% compared to S&P 500 (SPY) losses of nearly 10% since my last evaluation in September 2024.

Back then, I reviewed ISRG’s Q2 2024 earnings, which saw revenue grow over $2 billion. The company was rolling out da Vinci 5, the latest iteration of its popular robotic surgical system. I liked ISRG because, once installed, its surgical systems generate predictable and significant revenue from sales of “Instruments and Accessories” and “Services.” Notably, once customers have the machine, switching is difficult. This makes ISRG sticky. However, I did note some risks like rising competition from other large players like Medtronic (MDT) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ).

All in all, due to ISRG’s head start in this burgeoning market, I rated ISRG a Buy despite a premium valuation.

The company has since reported full year 2024 and Q4 earnings, so let’s take another look!

Earnings

Q4 revenue grew 25% year-over-year to reach $2.41 billion. This was driven by growth in all three segments. Instruments and Accessories grew 23% to $1.41 billion. Remember, this represents ISRG’s recurring revenue. Recurring revenue constituted 84% of total revenue in 2024. Systems revenue was $655 million. The total da Vinci installed base grew 15% year-over-year to 9,902 systems by the end of last year. Finally, Services revenue was $347.4 million.

ISRG noted 18% growth in da Vinci procedure volume. This figure is consistent with overall robotic surgery market trends. Recall that this market is projected to grow at a CAGR of up to 22%.

Gross profit was $1.64 billion (68% margin). Net income was $686 million ($1.88 per share). ISRG free cash flow margin for Q4 was 21.2%. Notably, its capital expenditures have been higher.

This is related to the da Vinci 5’s March 2024 launch. Normally, ISRG sports FCF margins closer to 25%.

Looking ahead to 2025, ISRG envisions slight deceleration in worldwide da Vinci procedures (17% in 2024 to 13% to 16% in 2025) due to challenges posed in China (competition, government policies) and continued GLP-1 impact on bariatrics. While bariatric surgery represents a small portion of overall surgeries, it’s large enough to have some kind of an impact. Gross margins are also expected to take a hit (from 69.1% in 2024 to between 67% and 68% in 2025) due to a number of factors such as product mix and currency impacts. ISRG also expects operating expenses to increase from 10% in 2024 to 10% to 15% in 2025. This is related to depreciation from new facilities and R&D investments supporting da Vinci 5.

Regarding the da Vinci 5, its launch is going well. There were a total of 362 da Vinci 5 placements in 2024.

Financial Health

As of December 31, ISRG held $4.013 billion in cash and short-term investments. Total current assets were $7.111 billion, while total current liabilities were just $1.745 billion. This implies that ISRG has solid liquidity in the next twelve months. The company is without any significant long term, interest-bearing debts and has accumulated $16.433 billion in total common equity.

Valuation Assumptions

As I’ve noted in the past, ISRG is priced optimistically (to say the least). Despite revenue growth (17.24% versus 6.92%) 149% greater than the sector median, ISRG multiples trade over four times its peers.

In the discounted cash flow (DCF) model that follows, I assume that ISRG’s FCF margins will average 25% over the nine-year projection period. I also modeled for 20% revenue growth, which would exceed its recent historical performance, but remains within reason given its leadership position in a booming market.

So, ISRG appears considerably overvalued on a cash flow basis, too. This is despite pretty optimistic assumptions.

The following sensitivity analysis acts as a stress test and reveals how changes to key assumptions impact valuation.

Essentially, ISRG appears priced for 26.7% year-over-year revenue growth assuming margins are stable at 25%. This is a really lofty expectation given increased competitive threats, historical growth, and projected future growth for the overall market.

Next, my Monte Carlo simulation adjusts for variability among a few key inputs like growth, margins, beta, and terminal growth. This provides a more dynamic and realistic view of share price outcomes.

As you can see, although ISRG is overvalued, it is not yet beyond reason. It’s close, though.

As I always point out, my primary interest is not whether a particular stock is overvalued/undervalued based on my assumptions, but if the market’s implied expectations are within reason. So, I do not just go off of whatever my DCF analysis tells me. Stocks are (apparently) overvalued and undervalued often (for good reasons). Models are models and they are subject to bias. Moreover, garbage in = garbage out. That is, if any one of my assumptions is dead wrong, it can throw off the results (although, this is one reason I run a Monte Carlo simulation).

On one hand, we have ISRG’s dominant market leadership in this massive market with tailwinds (e.g., growing adoption of robotic-assisted surgery), large and growing da Vinci installed base, significant recurring revenue stream, proven technology, and continuous innovation pipeline. This is worth a lot and why I assigned ISRG a terminal value of 4%, which is very generous. Revenue growth of 20% implies that ISRG hasn't come close to reaching market saturation and is unlikely to lose any market share to competitors.

On the other hand, competition is intensifying (which could lead to pricing pressure), macroeconomic headwinds could impede hospital capital spending, and tariffs pose risks to gross margins. On China, recall that about one-third of all ISRG revenue is from outside the U.S. China is a small, but noteworthy, portion of this (the exact amount is unknown). There are a few China-based robotic surgery companies that figure to have an edge in this market in the long-term, especially given the current macroeconomic environment. Chinese customers will likely gradually prefer domestic solutions for a number of reasons (e.g., cost). It's difficult to envision ISRG being a clear leader in China. For reference, look at Tesla's (TSLA) dwindling market share in China within the electrical vehicle market despite its head start.

Conclusion

In conclusion, in light of recent macroeconomic developments (tariffs, increasing change of recession, China-U.S. trade war), competition, and a premium stock valuation, I think it’s prudent to downgrade ISRG to Hold. The company is facing a number of headwinds (margin pressure, competition, procedure growth declaration) in 2025. Although these are projected to cause only modest changes, it could become “death by a thousand cuts” for a stock that is valued so highly. All things considered, it’s difficult to envision ISRG living up to the lofty growth and margin assumptions that are implied by its current valuation.

data.title: Conagra Brands: Likely To Miss FY26 EPS Estimates
data.description:
Investment Overview

I give a sell rating for Conagra Brands (NYSE:CAG). While valuation is already at ~10x forward PE today, I see potential for further de-rating when CAG misses estimates. Specifically, I expect margins to see more headwinds due to tariffs, which will pressure earnings growth, making it miss consensus FY26 estimates.

Business Description

CAG is a consumer-packaged foods [CPG] company that operates primarily in North America. It has four operating segments: Grocery & Snacks [G&S], Refrigerated & Frozen [R&F], International, and Others. Of the four, G&S and R&F are the largest, representing 44% and 39% of total sales, respectively. Through these business units, CAG sells a variety of items, including frozen meals, snacks, and pantry staples.

3Q25 Earnings Numbers Are Weak

CAG 3Q25 results headline numbers were clearly disappointing. Organic sales declined 5.2% y/y, with volume falling by 3.1% y/y and price/mix down 2.1% y/y. At the segment level, performance was weak across the board. Grocery & Snacks saw a 3.9% organic sales decline; Refrigerated & Frozen organic sales declined 7.2% y/y; International and Foodservice were also down 1.2% and 6.3% y/y, respectively. Total net sales came in at $2.84 billion on a reported basis, down 6.4% y/y. The decline led to gross margin contracting by 390 bps y/y to 24.8%. While CAG did an alright job in managing SG&A expense, coming in at $342 million vs. $371 million in 3Q24, this wasn't enough to offset the impact of lower gross profit (coming in at $703.9 million vs. $869.5 million in 3Q24). As a result, the EBIT margin contracted by 370 bps y/y to 12.7%.

Margin Erosion and Tariff Risks

What concerns me most is much more than just weak headline numbers, but the underlying reason for this poor performance. If it was a blip that can be solved easily, I wouldn't be too worried. The issue is, I don't think it is. I believe CAG is going to see much more margin pressure ahead, and I say this because input cost inflation continues to be a pain—it was 4% in 3Q25, and management noted cost inflation will remain at this level in FY26. While they said this was within expectations, I see room for downside because of the tariff situation, which management doesn't seem to have incorporated into their guide yet.

As a reminder, CAG has heavy sourcing exposure to tinplate steel, palm oil, and Mexican-grown vegetables, and all of them are vulnerable to incremental tariff duties. The saving grace is that CAG has a short timing window to find ways to resolve this impact because the tariffs won't fully hit until the current inventory turns over. But this window is small, in my opinion, as CAG is already seeing incremental cost from 3P co-manufacturers in 3Q25. As of 3Q25, CAG's days inventory outstanding is 47, which is about 1.5 months. And since CAG will need to replenish inventory in advance, I think CAG really only has until the mid-or-late of April 25 to solve this problem—which seems very unlikely to me. While there is the pricing lever that CAG can pull—like what they intend to do with Hebrew National in 4Q25—I think it is going to be a very difficult act given that consumers are in value-conscious mode. If CAG cannot raise prices effectively, and input costs inflate by a lot more, CAG margins are very likely to compress further.

To make things worse, CAG's balance sheet is not in the best shape. As of 3Q25, net debt-to-EBITDA leverage is ~3.6x, and management continues to prioritize dividend payout. This leaves little flexibility for CAG to swallow the margin hit (i.e., absorbing tariff cost inflation) to capture more volume (which may more than offset the tariff impact).

Consumption Trend Showed Improvement Sequentially in 3Q25

In the call, management noted that consumption trends improved sequentially in 3Q25, with volume up 1.1% y/y and dollar sales down just 0.1% y/y. The strength was seen across both the largest segments: Snacks saw volume growing 3.8% y/y, and R&F saw 1.3% y/y volume growth (did better than expected on a sell-through basis). That said, I am still not fully convinced that consumption trends are going to keep moving upwards from here. I think there is some pull-forward impact, wherein consumers are stocking up their pantry to avoid the price hikes from tariffs.

Valuation

In my opinion, the market has yet to fully price in the upcoming earnings weakness. While the stock now trades at just 10x forward PE, far below its historical average of 14x, and is below the low end of its trading band (11.4x), the problem I see here is consensus is anchoring their FY25/FY26 estimates against management's reiterated $2.35 FY25 EPS guidance, with the FY26 EPS estimate implying positive EPS growth. I think CAG will not be able to achieve this if tariffs hit CAG's cost structure as I expected. Note that EPS has not grown year-to-date in FY25, and this is before the tariff cost shows up in CAG's balance sheet. If things go south, we could see valuation drop further.

Upside Risk

Consumption trends could prove more resilient than I expected, and CAG may outperform my expectations. In particular, if CAG is able to balance price increases and volume gain/loss, it may be able to offset any margin headwinds, leading to positive EPS growth. I think the market will react very positively in this case, and valuation may see a positive re-rating.

Conclusion

I give a sell rating for CAG. While the stock appears cheap on a headline forward PE basis, I believe the market may have underestimated the impact of tariffs on CAG's cost structure. With limited pricing flexibility, elevated leverage, and consensus still pricing in FY26 EPS growth, I see downside risk to both earnings and valuation. Unless CAG can materially outperform on volume or execute pricing, I see more downside from here.

data.title: Conventum-Alluvium Global Fund March 2025 Quarterly Report
data.description:
Introduction

We are writing this on April 7th, just days after Trump’s “Liberation Day”. What a calamity! The Index is down 10.4% in two days (in EUR terms) 1, 2. The “Trump Bump” has turned into a “Trump Slump”. And to think just a few months it seemed universally accepted that his re-election would be positive for markets. How quickly times have changed. It’s anyone’s guess as to global growth ramiﬁcations - but it surely will not be positive and equity prices should suffer. However we are sure Trump and tariffs are entirely to blame for the sell-off. As US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent points out, markets peaked in mid-February. 3 We suspect that investors needed good reason to catalyse a correction - which many punters felt was inevitable.

Even after the sell-off, you don’t have to go back too far to see that returns from investing in richly priced stocks have been extraordinary. The following table puts a few things in perspective.

For the quarter, the Fund was up 1.7% in USD terms and 1.1% in AUD terms, but with a strengthening currency in EUR terms it was down 2.4% 4. Snapshot

  • As we try to assimilate the ever changing Trump directives, and we digest and process company’s full year earnings, we were decidedly quiet on the trading front. In fact, we only made one trade.
  • Alibaba (BABA) was the bright spark, with a return of 55.3%. Management’s conﬁdence in its cloud / AI business appears the main catalyst, although as we discuss later, it is likely other factors played a role too.
  • Healthcare did well, with McKesson (MCK), the drug distributor, up another 18.2%. HCA Healthcare (HCA), the hospital owner, largely recovered from last quarter’s dismal performance, and was up 15.4%.
  • Concerns over Trump’s tariffs caused a noticeable drop off in business conﬁdence through the quarter. How can one possibly make investment plans when the goal posts keep moving?
  • Robert Half, (RHI, the recruiter), and Thor (THO, the recreational vehicle producer) are awaiting rebounds in business and consumer conﬁdence. With the opposite occurring, their share prices suffered. They were down 21.8% and 20.4%.
  • Alphabet (GOOG,GOOGL), ie Google, was down 18.2% (we suspect it was just caught up in “Mag 7” demise, and Linamar (OTCPK:LIMAF), being particularly hit by proposed tariffs, was down 11.8%. Dick’s (DKS) was down 11.4%.
  • We hold the same 20 positions and ended quarter with 18.9% cash.
